sentences.base_language: Khalid al-Omari is a programmer and works a lot during the week.
sentences.target_language: خالد العمري مبرمجٌ ويعمل كثيراً خلال الأسبوع.
sentences.romanized: Khalid al-'Omari mubarammijun wa-ya'mal kathiran khilal al-usbua'.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Zero copula** — Arabic does not use a verb 'to be' in the present tense: خالد **مبرمجٌ** (Khalid [is] a programmer). The subject and predicate stand side by side. Compare the past: خالد **كان** مبرمجاً (Khalid **was** a programmer) — **كان** appears only in past and future.
**تنوين** (tanwin, nunation): the suffix **ٌ** on مبرمجٌ marks the indefinite nominative — the '...un' sound. Arabic nouns carry case and definiteness: مبرمجٌ (indefinite nominative), المبرمجُ (definite nominative). In everyday spoken Arabic, tanwin is often dropped.

sentences.base_language: On Saturday morning, he woke up at seven.
sentences.target_language: في صباح يوم السبت، استيقظ في الساعة السابعة.
sentences.romanized: Fi sabah yawm as-sabt, istayqaza fi as-sa'a as-sabi'a.
sentences.grammarNote:
**استيقظ** (istayqaza) = woke up — Form X verb (استَفعَلَ pattern): Form X typically expresses requesting or achieving a state. The ten verb forms (أوزان, awzan) are the backbone of Arabic morphology — knowing the form predicts the meaning type.
**الساعة السابعة** = the seventh hour (7:00) — Arabic tells time with ordinal numbers: الساعة **الأولى** (1:00), **الثانية** (2:00), **السابعة** (7:00). The definite article **ال** (al-) on both noun and adjective marks them as a definite noun phrase — adjectives must agree in definiteness, gender, and number with their noun.

sentences.base_language: He was very hungry and wanted to go to the grocery store.
sentences.target_language: كان جائعاً جداً وأراد الذهاب إلى السوبرماركت.
sentences.romanized: Kana ja'i'an jiddan wa-arada adh-dhihab ila as-supirmarkit.
sentences.grammarNote:
**كان** + predicate in accusative: كان **جائعاً** (he was hungry — جائعاً is accusative, marked by -an/ً). The copula **كان** governs its predicate in the accusative case. This كان-construction is essential: كان سعيداً (he was happy), كانت متعبةً (she was tired).
**الذهاب** = the going — the **مصدر** (masdar, verbal noun) acts as a noun: أراد **الذهاب** (wanted the going = wanted to go). Arabic uses verbal nouns after prepositions and as objects, where English uses infinitives or gerunds. **المصدر** is the base form from which verb patterns are built.

sentences.base_language: He washed his face, got dressed, and left home.
sentences.target_language: غسل وجهه وارتدى ملابسه وخرج من المنزل.
sentences.romanized: Ghsala wajhahu wa-rtada malabisahu wa-kharaja mina al-manzil.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Possessive suffixes (ضمائر اتصال)**: وجه**ه** = his face, ملابس**ه** = his clothes — the suffix ـه (-hu) means 'his'. These enclitic pronouns attach to nouns: بيت**ي** (my house), كتاب**ك** (your book), أمّ**ه** (his mother). No separate possessive word needed — the suffix carries all the information.
**ارتدى** = wore/put on — Form VIII or a separate verb of wearing. **خرج من** (kharaja min) = exited from — **من** (min) marks the source/origin: خرج مِن المنزل (left the house), جاء مِن بغداد (came from Baghdad).

sentences.base_language: The grocery store was nearby — only a ten-minute walk.
sentences.target_language: كان السوبرماركت قريباً — على بُعد عشر دقائق سيراً على الأقدام.
sentences.romanized: Kana as-supirmarkit qariban — 'ala bu'd 'ashr daqa'iq sayran 'ala al-aqdaм.
sentences.grammarNote:
**على بُعد** = at a distance of: على بُعد **كيلومترين** (two kilometers away), على بُعد **ساعة** (an hour away). **بُعد** (bu'd) = distance (from بَعُدَ, to be far).
**سيراً على الأقدام** = on foot, lit. 'walking on the feet' — a formal Arabic phrase for 'on foot.' The **ً** ending on سيراً is the accusative indefinite (as a **حال**, haal — a circumstantial adverbial describing the manner of being). **الأقدام** is the broken plural of **قدم** (foot): a classic Arabic plural form that changes the internal vowel pattern rather than adding a suffix.

sentences.base_language: There he bought eggs, vegetables, and chicken.
sentences.target_language: هناك اشترى بيضاً وخضروات ودجاجاً.
sentences.romanized: Hunaka ishtara baydan wa-khadrawat wa-dajajen.
sentences.grammarNote:
**اشترى** (ishtara) = bought — Form VIII verb (افتَعَلَ pattern), past tense 3rd person masculine singular. The final vowel on Form VIII past tense 3rd person: اشتر**ى** (ending in alif maqsura). Verbs ending in ى or و are called **أفعال ناقصة** (defective verbs) and follow special conjugation rules.
**Accusative after verbs**: بيضاً, دجاجاً are indefinite accusative (tanwin fath, -an) — direct objects of **اشترى**. **خضروات** is a جمع مؤنث سالم (sound feminine plural, -ات), which is not marked with tanwin in the accusative in formal Arabic.

sentences.base_language: He also grabbed some fruit: apples and bananas.
sentences.target_language: أخذ أيضاً بعض الفاكهة: تفاحاً وموزاً.
sentences.romanized: Akhadha aydan ba'da al-fakiha: tuffahan wa-mawzan.
sentences.grammarNote:
**بعض + definite noun** = some of: **بعض** الفاكهة (some of the fruit), **بعض** الناس (some people). **بعض** is always followed by a definite genitive noun — the noun after بعض takes **كسرة** (kasra, -i) as a genitive:
**إضافة** (idafa, construct state): when two nouns are chained (possessive or partitive), the first loses its **ال** and takes genitive: بعض + الفاكهة → بعض الفاكهة. Arabic noun chains (إضافة) are extremely frequent: بيت الطالب (the student's house), كتاب العلم (the book of science).

sentences.base_language: After finishing, he sent his friend a message.
sentences.target_language: بعد انتهاء التسوق، أرسل رسالةً إلى صديقه.
sentences.romanized: Ba'da intiha' at-tasawwuq, arsala risalatan ila sadiqihi.
sentences.grammarNote:
**بعد + مصدر** (verbal noun): بعد **انتهاء** التسوق (after the end of shopping = after finishing shopping). Arabic expresses 'after doing' using **بعد** + verbal noun (مصدر) — not an infinitive. This is one of the most essential Arabic grammatical patterns: قبل **البداية** (before the start), بعد **الوصول** (after arriving).
**إلى صديقه** = to his friend — **إلى** (to/toward) marks direction. Possessive suffix: صديق**ه** (his friend). **أرسل** (arsala) = sent — Form IV verb (أَفعَل): often causative in meaning — أرسَلَ (caused to go = sent).

sentences.base_language: His friend replied: "I am waiting for you at the café!"
sentences.target_language: ردَّ صديقُه: «أنا أنتظرك في المقهى!»
sentences.romanized: Radda sadiquhu: «Ana antaziruka fi al-maqha!»
sentences.grammarNote:
**أنتظرك** = I am waiting for you — verb + attached object pronoun: أنتظر (I wait) + **ك** (-ka, you masc.) → أنتظر**ك**. Object pronouns attach directly to verbs: أحبّ**ك** (I love you), يساعد**ك** (he helps you), أرسلت**ه** (I sent it). These enclitic pronouns change slightly depending on what they follow.
**«»** — Arabic quotation marks (guillemets). **المقهى** (al-maqha) = the café — a word borrowed from the Turkish kahvehane or from قهوة (qahwa, coffee). Many modern Arabic words are loanwords rendered in Arabic script.

sentences.base_language: Khalid left the bags at home and went to the café.
sentences.target_language: ترك خالد الأكياس في البيت وذهب إلى المقهى.
sentences.romanized: Taraka Khalid al-akyas fi al-bayt wa-dhahaba ila al-maqha.
sentences.grammarNote:
**VSO word order** — Arabic classically places the verb first: **ترك** خالد (left Khalid = Khalid left). When the subject comes before the verb (SVO), it's also common in Modern Standard and spoken Arabic. In VSO sentences, the verb does not agree in number with the subject — it stays singular: **ترك** خالد (not *تركوا*); agreement is added when the subject precedes.
**الأكياس** = the bags — definite plural: كيس (bag) → أكياس (broken plural). This is a classic Arabic broken plural (جمع تكسير) where the internal vowel structure changes completely. Learning broken plurals is one of the major challenges of Arabic.

sentences.base_language: His friend Sara was already sitting there, with a smile on her face.
sentences.target_language: كانت صديقته سارة جالسةً هناك بالفعل، وعلى وجهها ابتسامة.
sentences.romanized: Kanat sadiqatuhu Sara jalisatan hunaka bil-fi'l, wa-'ala wajhiha ibtisama.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Feminine agreement**: كانت (kānat) is the feminine past of كان — Arabic verbs agree in gender. كانَ (he was) → كانَ**ت** (she was). جالس (sitting, masc.) → جالسة (sitting, fem.) + ً accusative tanwin → جالسةً.
**بالفعل** = already / indeed, lit. 'by the action' — a useful discourse particle. **على وجهها** = on her face — possessive suffix ها (-ha, her): وجه (face) + ها → وجه**ها** (her face). Note the Arabic script: the ها suffix attaches directly: وجهها. This وجهها is in the genitive (after على), which in Arabic shows on pronouns but not always on the noun in spoken Arabic.

sentences.base_language: Sara asked him: "How are you? You look tired."
sentences.target_language: سألته سارة: «كيف حالك؟ تبدو متعباً.»
sentences.romanized: Sa'alathu Sara: «Kayfa haluk? Tabdu muta'aban.»
sentences.grammarNote:
**سألته** = asked him — verb (سأل) + object suffix (ه): سألَ**ته** (asked + him). When a verb has a direct-object suffix, the suffix comes immediately after the verb stem: كلّمته (spoke to him), ساعدته (helped him).
**كيف حالك؟** = how are you? lit. 'how is your state/condition?' — **حال** (hal) = condition/state; **ك** = your. A very common greeting. Responses: بخير (fine), الحمد لله (praise be to God — common positive response), تمام (great). **تبدو** (tabdu) = you look/appear (Form I, present 2nd masc. sing. of بدا).

sentences.base_language: Khalid sighed and said: "Work exhausts me — there is a lot of pressure."
sentences.target_language: تنهَّد خالد وقال: «العمل يُجهدني — هناك ضغطٌ كبير.»
sentences.romanized: Tannahhada Khalid wa-qala: «Al-'amal yujhiduni — hunaka daghton kabir.»
sentences.grammarNote:
**تنهَّد** = sighed — Form V verb (تَفعَّلَ pattern), often reflexive or intensive: تَعلَّم (learned, studied intensively), تَكلَّم (spoke), تنهَّد (sighed deeply). Form V is frequently the reflexive of Form II.
**يُجهدني** = exhausts me — Form IV verb (أجهَد, to exhaust/fatigue) + ني (me): يُجهد + ني → يُجهدني. The first person object suffix is ني after a verb. **هناك** = there is (existential): هناك كتاب (there is a book). Arabic uses هناك for both 'there (location)' and 'there is.' **ضغط** (daght) = pressure — used for stress in Modern Arabic.

sentences.base_language: Sara said: "You need to relax! Let's go for a little walk in the park."
sentences.target_language: قالت سارة: «تحتاج إلى الاسترخاء! هيّا نتمشّى قليلاً في الحديقة.»
sentences.romanized: Qalat Sara: «Tahtaju ila al-istirkha'! Hayya natamashsha qalilan fi al-hadiqa.»
sentences.grammarNote:
**تحتاج إلى** = needs — Form VIII verb احتاج + إلى: احتاج **إلى** (need something — إلى marks what is needed). **الاسترخاء** is the مصدر (verbal noun) of Form X استرخى (to relax). After **إلى**, the noun is genitive: يحتاج إلى **الراحةِ** (needs rest).
**هيّا** = let's / come on — an Arabic particle for urging action: هيّا نذهب (let's go), هيّا بنا (let's go, with 'us'). **نتمشّى** is the present tense of Form V تمشّى (to stroll/walk leisurely), 1st person plural — used here as a hortative ('let's walk'). The حديقة (hadiqa) = park/garden.

sentences.base_language: After finishing their coffees, they both went to the park together.
sentences.target_language: بعد انتهائهما من القهوة، ذهبا معاً إلى الحديقة.
sentences.romanized: Ba'da intiha'ihima mina al-qahwa, dhahabba ma'an ila al-hadiqa.
sentences.grammarNote:
**المثنى** (dual): Arabic has a distinct dual form for exactly two of something. **ذهبا** = they two went — past tense dual: فعلَ → فعل**ا** (he went → they-two went). **انتهائهما** = their two's finishing — the dual possessive suffix ـهما (-huma): انتهاء + هما → انتهائ**هما** (their dual finishing). This suffix appears on nouns, adjectives, and verbs.
**انتهائهما** is an إضافة (construct chain): انتهاء (the finishing of) + هما (them two). The dual is used consistently in Arabic when exactly two persons or things are referenced — unlike English which uses 'they' for both two and more.

sentences.base_language: The park air was fresh — Khalid took a deep breath and felt much better.
sentences.target_language: كان هواء الحديقة منعشاً — تنفَّس خالد بعمق وشعر بتحسُّن كبير.
sentences.romanized: Kana hawa' al-hadiqa mun'ishan — tanaffasa Khalid bi-'umq wa-sha'ara bi-tahassun kabir.
sentences.grammarNote:
**هواء الحديقة** = the park's air — إضافة (construct state): هواء (air of) + الحديقة (the park) → air-of-the-park. In an إضافة, the first noun loses the article and case is marked; the second is in the genitive. Arabic expresses possession and 'of' relationships exclusively through إضافة.
**تنفَّس** = breathed — Form V (تَفعَّل) of نَفَس (breath): تنفَّس (inhaled and exhaled). **بعمق** = deeply — preposition **بـ** (bi) + عمق (depth): with depth/deeply. **شعر بـ** = felt — يشعر بـ (to feel something): شعر بالسعادة (felt happiness), شعر بالتعب (felt tired). **بـ** introduces the feeling.

sentences.base_language: They walked in the park for an hour and talked about everything.
sentences.target_language: مشيا في الحديقة لمدة ساعة وتحدَّثا عن كل شيء.
sentences.romanized: Mashaya fi al-hadiqa li-muddat sa'a wa-tahaddatha 'an kulli shay'.
literal: for a duration of an hour
sentences.grammarNote:
**Dual verbs**: مشيا (they two walked) and تحدَّثا (they two spoke) — past tense dual ends in **ا** (-a): مشى → مشي**ا**, تحدَّث → تحدَّث**ا**. In the present tense, the dual ends in **ان** (-an): يمشيان (they two walk), يتحدَّثان (they two speak). The dual is consistently maintained throughout Arabic grammar.
**لمدة** = for a duration of — **لمدة** (li-muddat) + time noun is the standard way to express 'for [time]': لمدة **ساعة** (for an hour), لمدة **أسبوع** (for a week). **كل شيء** = everything, lit. 'every thing': **كل** (every/all) + **شيء** (thing).

sentences.base_language: On the way home, Khalid's body was a little sore, but his mood was very good.
sentences.target_language: في الطريق إلى المنزل، كان جسم خالد مؤلماً قليلاً، لكن مزاجه كان جيداً جداً.
sentences.romanized: Fi at-tariq ila al-manzil, kana jism Khalid mu'liman qalilan, lakin mizajahu kana jayyidan jiddan.
sentences.grammarNote:
**جسم خالد** = Khalid's body — إضافة (construct): جسم (body of) + خالد → the body of Khalid. Arabic expresses 'X's Y' as Y-of-X in إضافة. The first noun carries no article; the proper noun خالد is inherently definite, making the whole إضافة definite.
**مزاجه** = his mood — مزاج + ه (-hu, his). **كان جيداً جداً** — note **جداً** (very, greatly) always comes after the adjective: جيد **جداً** (very good), كبير **جداً** (very big). In Arabic, **جداً** is post-adjective, unlike English 'very' which precedes.

sentences.base_language: He decided to go out with friends every weekend from now on.
sentences.target_language: قرَّر الخروج مع الأصدقاء كلَّ نهاية أسبوع من الآن فصاعداً.
sentences.romanized: Qarrara al-khuruj ma'a al-asdiqa' kulla nihayat usbua' min al-an fasa'idan.
sentences.grammarNote:
**قرَّر** (qarrara) = decided — Form II verb (فَعَّل, doubled middle consonant): Form II is often causative or intensive: درَّس (taught), علَّم (educated), قرَّر (decided/resolved). **الخروج** = the going out — مصدر of خرج (Form I): الخروج is the definite verbal noun, used as the object of قرَّر (decided on going out).
**من الآن فصاعداً** = from now on — **فصاعداً** is a frozen form meaning 'and upward/onward': من الآن **فصاعداً** (from now onward). A fixed classical expression. **الأصدقاء** (al-asdiqa') = the friends — broken plural of صديق (sadiq, friend): one of many unpredictable broken plural patterns.

sentences.base_language: That night, he fell asleep very quickly and slept a wonderful sleep.
sentences.target_language: في تلك الليلة، نام بسرعة كبيرة ونام نوماً هنيئاً.
sentences.romanized: Fi tilka al-layla, nama bi-sur'a kabira wa-nama nawman hani'an.
sentences.grammarNote:
**تلك** = that (fem.) — Arabic demonstratives agree in gender: هذا (this, masc.), هذه (this, fem.), ذلك (that, masc.), تلك (that, fem.). **الليلة** (al-layla) is feminine, so **تلك** is correct. Knowing noun gender is essential for choosing the right demonstrative and adjective forms.
**نوماً هنيئاً** — the **مفعول مطلق** (cognate accusative): نام **نوماً** (slept a sleep) reinforces the verb with its own verbal noun. This classical Arabic construction adds emphasis or describes the manner of the action: عمل عملاً جيداً (worked a good work = worked well), بكى بكاءً (wept bitterly). **هنيئاً** = pleasant/restful (as an adjective modifying نوماً).
